text:
Great question! You have two solid options for Next.js. [1]

## Option A: Static Files (Simplest)

Place the files directly in your `/public` directory at the root. Next.js will serve them automatically:

```
your-nextjs-project/
├── public/
│   ├── llms.txt
│   ├── llms-full.txt
│   └── .well-known/
│       └── ucp.json
└── ...
```

Then download the generated files from the Setup tab here, and commit them to your repo. They'll be accessible at:
- `https://example.com/llms.txt`
- `https://example.com/llms-full.txt`
- `https://example.com/.well-known/ucp.json`

## Option B: Proxy Route (Recommended)

If you prefer to keep files auto-updated without re-deploying, set up a proxy route. Create API routes in Next.js that redirect to FlowBlinq: [1]

- `https://example.com/llms.txt` → `https://geo.flowblinq.com/api/serve/{your-slug}/llms.txt`
- `https://example.com/llms-full.txt` → `https://geo.flowblinq.com/api/serve/{your-slug}/llms-full.txt`
- `https://example.com/.well-known/ucp.json` → `https://geo.flowblinq.com/api/serve/{your-slug}/business.json`

(Replace `{your-slug}` with your domain slug from the Setup tab.)

**Which should you pick?**
- **Static files** if you want zero dependencies and full control
- **Proxy** if you want files to auto-update when you re-run audits

Which approach feels better for your setup?
